WhatWeDoPage.title: What We Do
WhatWeDoPage.description: From sketch to pattern, instantly. See how Sewrio's AI turns your designs into precise, production-ready sewing patterns.
WhatWeDoPage.heading: From sketch to pattern, instantly.

steps.title: Upload your photo
steps.description: Upload a photo of the garment you want to recreate. Sewrio examines the image and turns it into a structured pattern draft within minutes.
steps.alt: Uploading a photo of a garment design

steps.title: See it before you cut
steps.description: Review your pattern on screen before you cut. Seam allowance, straight grain and measurements are all in place — edit them if you need to.

steps.title: Export production files
steps.description: Download your finished pattern in the formats used in production and go straight to sewing.

WhyUsSection.heading: Built by people who sew.
WhyUsSection.intro: Sewrio works differently: it starts from a photo you already have and turns it into a sewing pattern you can work with. Instead of looking at a garment and sketching out what its pattern might look like, you hand over the photo and get the pattern ready-made.
points: Produces a sewing pattern directly from a photo.
Delivers results in seconds, removing days of manual drafting.
No training required — unlike CAD software that takes weeks to learn.
Gives you an editable pattern, not a static image.
Preserves technical details like seam allowance and straight grain.
Exports to the formats used in production.
WhyUsSection.closing: CAD software is powerful once you're drawing — Sewrio shortens the path to get there, turning the idea in your photo into a usable pattern quickly, with no learning curve.

items.q: What can I do with the demo?
items.a: Experience SEWRIO's workflow by uploading a garment photo and generating a basic digital sewing pattern. The demo is designed to showcase the platform and includes daily upload limits and simplified pattern generation.

items.q: What kind of clothing photos work best?
items.a: For the best results, upload a flat-lay image with a clean background, the entire garment visible, and minimal folds or wrinkles. Simple garments such as t-shirts, dresses, skirts, and shirts currently produce the most accurate patterns.

items.q: What file formats does SEWRIO export?
items.a: Generated patterns can be exported as PDF and PNG files, making them easy to print, review, or use during the pattern-making process. Additional professional export formats such as DXF and SVG will be available in future releases.

items.q: Do I need to install anything?
items.a: No. SEWRIO runs entirely in your browser. There's nothing to download or install—simply upload your garment image and start generating patterns.

items.q: Do I need to sign up?
items.a: Yes. A valid email address is required to access the demo. No credit card is needed, and you'll receive updates as new features and the full platform become available.

items.q: Does my pattern come in my size?
items.a: The current demo generates a standard-size pattern for demonstration purposes. Custom body measurements, made-to-measure pattern generation, and automatic grading will be available in the full version.
Yes. Your uploaded images and generated patterns remain private. We do not use your files to train AI models without your explicit permission.

items.q: Can I edit the generated pattern?
items.a: The demo provides a preview of the generated pattern. Full editing tools, measurement adjustments, and advanced customization will be included in the complete platform.

The demo performs best with simple garment types such as dresses, skirts, shirts, and t-shirts. Support for jackets, trousers, hoodies, and more complex garment constructions is continuously being expanded.

items.q: How accurate are the generated patterns?
items.a: The demo is intended to demonstrate SEWRIO's AI workflow rather than replace professional pattern development. Pattern quality will continue to improve as the platform evolves and additional AI models are introduced.

why-i-started-building-sewrio.title: Why I started building Sewrio
why-i-started-building-sewrio.excerpt: It started with a dress I couldn't buy and a custom sewing pattern that cost more than the dress itself. Here's how a frustrating personal problem became an AI pattern-generation platform.
content: People often ask why I decided to build an AI-powered pattern generation platform. The truth is, I never planned to start a fashion technology company. It all began with a dress.
I'd found a beautiful dress from a brand overseas, exactly what I was looking for. The company didn't ship to Turkey, and nothing else came close. So I thought: what if I just sewed it myself?

I don't have professional pattern-making skills, so I asked someone to draft the sewing pattern from photos. The price shocked me: a single custom pattern cost more than the dress itself, enough to make me question whether sewing it was even worth it. It felt strange. AI can generate images, write code, compose music, even help discover new medicines, yet turning a garment photo into a usable sewing pattern was still an expensive, manual service.

With a background in molecular biology, genetics, and engineering, my instinct was simple: surely AI can solve this. I tested existing tools, image-processing pipelines, and computer vision models. Some could identify clothing. Some could segment garments. Some could estimate shapes. None could generate something you could actually cut from fabric and sew.

That's when I realized the real challenge wasn't recognizing clothes. It was understanding how garments are constructed. A sewing pattern isn't just an outline; it encodes proportions, construction logic, ease allowances, curves, seam relationships, and decades of grading knowledge. It wasn't an image-recognition problem. It was a translation problem: turning a 2D photo into a manufacturable digital pattern.

I started building tools just for myself, to remove the repetitive work. Then I realized thousands of makers, fashion students, independent designers, and small businesses hit the exact same wall: hours drafting by hand, high fees for custom work, or giving up on a design entirely. That's when the project stopped being a personal experiment and became Sewrio.

Sewrio is our attempt to make digital pattern creation accessible to everyone, combining computer vision, AI, and parametric pattern generation to remove one of the biggest barriers in garment creation. The goal isn't to replace pattern makers. It's to give creators a smarter starting point, so they spend less time on repetitive technical work and more time designing. The dress that couldn't be shipped to Turkey became the start of an entirely new journey. Sometimes the best products don't begin with a brilliant business idea; they begin with a frustrating personal problem. Sewrio started with mine.

how-to-read-a-sewing-pattern-beginners-g.title: How to Read a Sewing Pattern (Beginner's Guide)
how-to-read-a-sewing-pattern-beginners-g.excerpt: Sewing pattern symbols, lines, and markings explained simply, so you can go from envelope to cutting table without guessing.
content: Every sewing pattern is covered in lines, symbols, and abbreviations that look confusing the first time you open one. Once you know what each mark means, though, a pattern stops being a puzzle and starts being a set of instructions you can follow with confidence.

Start with the outer cutting line, usually a solid line that shows exactly where your scissors should go. Just inside it, a dashed line marks the seam line, the point where your stitches will actually sit once the seam allowance is folded back.

Grainline arrows tell you how to place the pattern piece on your fabric so it hangs correctly once sewn; they should always run parallel to the selvedge. A 'fold' symbol, usually a bracket with arrows pointing to a line, means that piece is cut on a folded edge of fabric rather than as a single layer.

Notches, the small triangles or lines along the seam edge, exist purely to help you match pieces up correctly during construction. Circles and dots mark where darts start and end, where pockets attach, or where a zipper opening begins.

Sizing information, cutting layouts, and yardage requirements are usually printed separately on the pattern instruction sheet, along with a key explaining every symbol used. Reading through that key once before you start cutting saves far more time than guessing your way through a seam later.

how-to-print-pdf-sewing-patterns-at-home.title: How to Print PDF Sewing Patterns at Home
how-to-print-pdf-sewing-patterns-at-home.excerpt: A step-by-step guide to printing, checking, and taping together a PDF sewing pattern on a standard home printer.
content: PDF sewing patterns are usually formatted to print across multiple standard sheets of paper, tiled like a puzzle you assemble at home. Getting a clean, accurate result takes a few extra steps beyond just hitting print.

The single most important setting is scale. In your PDF viewer's print dialog, choose '100%' or 'Actual size', never 'Fit to page' or 'Shrink to fit', since either of those will scale the pattern down and throw off every measurement.

Almost every PDF pattern includes a test square, usually a 1 inch or 5 centimeter box printed on the first page. Print that page first, then measure the square with a ruler before printing the rest. If it doesn't match exactly, adjust your printer's scale setting and test again.

Once printing is confirmed accurate, print the remaining pages and trim the margins along the guide lines printed on each sheet. Lay the pages out in order using the small page numbers or a layout diagram included in the file, then tape them together, edge to edge, before cutting out your pattern pieces.

If your home printer only handles letter or A4 paper, look for a 'copy shop' or 'projector' version of the file when one is available. Copy shop files print on large-format paper at a print shop with no assembly required, and projector files let you trace the pattern directly onto fabric using a projector, skipping printing altogether.

digital-vs-printed-sewing-patterns-which.title: Digital vs Printed Sewing Patterns: Which One Is Better?
digital-vs-printed-sewing-patterns-which.excerpt: Cost, storage, editing, and convenience compared, so you can decide whether a digital or printed sewing pattern fits the way you actually sew.
content: Sewing patterns come in two basic formats today: printed patterns you buy as a physical envelope, and digital patterns you download and either print or trace yourself. Each has real advantages, and the better choice usually comes down to how you like to work rather than one format being objectively superior.

Printed patterns are ready to use the moment they arrive. There's no printing, taping, or scale-checking involved, and the tissue paper is often reusable across multiple projects if you're careful with it. The tradeoff is less flexibility: resizing a printed pattern means tracing or physically altering the tissue by hand.

Digital patterns cost less to produce and distribute, which is often reflected in a lower price, and they arrive instantly instead of waiting on shipping. Because the pattern exists as a file, resizing between included sizes takes seconds rather than an afternoon with tracing paper, and a layer-based PDF lets you print only the size you need.

The real cost of a digital pattern shows up at home: paper, ink, and the time spent taping sheets together, or a trip to a copy shop for a large-format print. For anyone who sews often, that one-time setup is a small price for a pattern library that never takes up shelf space.

If you sew occasionally and want something ready to cut into right away, a printed pattern is hard to beat. If you sew across a range of sizes, want to make quick fit adjustments, or would rather build a digital library than a drawer full of tissue, digital patterns are usually the better fit, especially as tools that generate them directly from a photo or sketch make the format even more convenient.
